parts.answer: Knowledge 1, Application 2, Analysis 1 Quantitative skills assessed: QS5: Calculate cost, revenue, profit and break-even Knowledge 1 mark for identifying the formula for calculating the decrease in revenue: • Old level of revenue – new level of revenue (Old number of passengers x price) – (New number of passengers x price) (1) Application Up to 2 marks for selecting the correct data: • (40 x $999) (1) - (29 x $999) (1) OR • $39 960 (1) - $28 971 (1) Analysis 1 mark for calculating the decrease in revenue: • $10 989 (1) NB: If no working is shown, award marks as follows: • If the answer given is $10 989 award 4 marks • If the answer given is 10 989 award 3 marks (4) Question Analyse two possible advantages for Green Tortoise of having limited liability. Mark Knowledge Up to 2 marks for defining limited liability, e.g. • When a business has a separate legal identity (1) to its owner(s) (1) OR For stating up to two advantages, e.g. • If the business fails or goes out of business, the owner(s) will only be liable to lose the money paid for their shares (1) • Shareholders have protection against legal claims on the business (1) Application Up to 2 marks for answers contextualised to Green Tortoise, e.g. • Maximum passenger capacity is now lower but prices have not increased (1) • A passenger may file a legal complaint if they became ill after preparation of meals on a bus tour (1) Analysis Up to 2 marks for reasons/causes/consequences for Green Tortoise, e.g. • Owner (Lyle Kent) would not have to sell personal assets to pay for debts of the business should it run into difficulties due to this change (1) • Owner (Lyle Kent) would not be sued personally if there is a legal claim where Green Tortoise is found liable (1) (6) Question Discuss the importance of developing customer loyalty for a business such as Green Tortoise. Indicative content (8)

Indicative content guidance Answers must be credited by using the level descriptors (below) in line with the general marking guidance. The indicative content below exemplifies some of the points that candidates may make but this does not imply that any of these must be included. Other relevant points must also be credited. Knowledge, Application, Analysis, Evaluation – indicative content • Customer loyalty means customers return to the business, therefore increasing sales and profitability • Customer loyalty can be developed by good communication, providing customer service, offering incentives, personalising the goods/services or offering preferential treatment • Green Tortoise has shown it pays attention to its customer service by reducing the maximum capacity to 29 on each bus tour • Tours are frequently adjusted to the preferences of those travelling, meaning it personalises the service offered • The method of preparing meals is quite unique for a tour business and is a further example of personalising its service • As a result, Green Tortoise is showing evidence of trying to develop customer loyalty and it has apparently been successful in the past, due to many customers returning to travel on other tours • However, capacity may have been reduced further if Green Tortoise’s main focus was on developing customer loyalty and, as it has been in business since 1973, it has waited a long time to reduce the number of passengers on a tour to 29 from 40 • As the percentage of repeat customers has been reducing, it is possible the focus moved away from development of customer loyalty • There may be enough new customers attracted to the tours to make them successful without having returning customers Level Mark Descriptor 0 No rewardable material. Level 1 1–2 Isolated elements of knowledge and understanding – recall based. Weak or no relevant application to business examples. Generic assertions may be presented. Level 2 3–5 Accurate knowledge and understanding. Applied a

Indicative content guidance Answers must be credited by using the level descriptors (below) in line with the general marking guidance. The indicative content below exemplifies some of the points that candidates may make but this does not imply that any of these must be included. Other relevant points must also be credited. Knowledge, Application, Analysis, Evaluation – indicative content • Revenue can be calculated by quantity of passengers x price • As the maximum number of passengers on a bus tour reduced by 11 per tour, this is likely to reduce the revenue for Green Tortoise considerably • An example would be losing $999 x 11 = $10 989 in revenue for one of its popular ‘Pacific Trek’ tours (a 27.5% decrease) • This means that Green Tortoise would need to reduce costs or be likely to experience a fall in profit • As 70% of meals are included in the price, the overall cost of ingredients is likely to reduce in line with the capacity of passengers, meaning that profit should not decrease as much as revenue • Acting upon customer feedback to provide more space may help to stop the percentage of repeat customers from decreasing • The impact on Green Tortoise may depend on how customers respond to the change in the quality and the comfort of the service • However, if they did increase the price this may deter some people from travelling with Green Tortoise • This is especially true as the business mainly targets travellers on a low budget and a higher price may not be as competitive against hostel-based accommodation and the least expensive bus operators in the US • As a result, Green Tortoise may lose customers and not be able to sell all the 29 tour places it has available • Therefore, although at a higher price, the lower number of passengers may mean revenue will be lower (depending on PED) • It may depend on the competition and alternative tours available to customers, as well as economic influences • This is especially true of a business that travels in other cou

Knowledge 1, Application 2, Analysis 1 Quantitative skills assessed: QS3: Construct and interpret arrange of standard graphical forms Knowledge 1 mark for identifying the formula: • Maximum inventory level – minimum inventory level (1) Application Up to 2 marks for selecting the correct data from Extract C: • 12 sacks (1) – 2 sacks (1) Analysis 1 mark for calculating the re-order quantity: • = 10 sacks (1) (4) Question The UK Government required many businesses to close their shops during the global health crisis. Some competitors of Golcar Fisheries did not survive this temporary closure. Analyse two possible reasons why this government legislation resulted in business failure for some of Golcar Fisheries’ competitors. Mark Knowledge Up to 2 marks for defining government legislation, e.g. • Legal requirements set by a government (1) which must be followed by individuals/businesses (1) OR For stating up to two possible reasons, e.g. • The legislation may limit what the business can do (1) • Businesses will have a lack of revenue to pay their costs (1) Application Up to 2 marks for answers contextualised to a business such as a competitor of Golcar Fisheries, e.g. • Shops were required to close for several months during the global health crisis (1) • Costs include perishable goods, such as sacks of potatoes (1) Analysis Up to 2 marks for reasons/causes/consequences for a business such as a competitor of Golcar Fisheries, e.g. • Therefore, the business would be unable to earn revenue from its usual activities, which could lead to business failure (1) • Costs of business still need to be paid in spite of receiving no income from customers coming into the shops (1) (6) Question Discuss the drawbacks to Golcar Fisheries of holding buffer inventories. Indicative content (8)

Indicative content guidance Answers must be credited by using the level descriptors (below) in line with the general marking guidance. The indicative content below exemplifies some of the points that candidates may make but this does not imply that any of these must be included. Other relevant points must also be credited. Knowledge, Application, Analysis, Evaluation – indicative content • A buffer inventory is an emergency inventory to help in case there is a shortage of inventory • Storing the inventory takes up space, meaning there could be limited room in the shop, especially as it is only a small shop • Not all the inventory will be sold and, as some products sold by Golcar Fisheries, such as chips (made from potatoes) are perishable goods, they may go out-of-date and be unable to be sold • This would cause a wastage cost to Golcar Fisheries which may be especially problematic at a time when the price of food is increasing • It would cost Golcar Fisheries more to hold buffer inventories, meaning less money would be available to spend on fuel costs, which have been increasing • However, if there are problems with delivery, such as transport getting delayed, Golcar Fisheries would still have inventory to sell • It would be able to meet a sudden increase in demand should there be more customers than usual • This means that regular customers would not be disappointed and the business would maintain it’s good reputation Level Mark Descriptor 0 No rewardable material. Level 1 1–2 Isolated elements of knowledge and understanding – recall based. Weak or no relevant application to business examples. Generic assertions may be presented. Level 2 3–5 Accurate knowledge and understanding. Applied accurately to the business and its context. Chains of reasoning are presented, showing cause(s) and/or effect(s) but may be assertions or incomplete. An attempt at an assessment is presented that is unbalanced, and unlikely to show the significance of competing arguments. Level 3 6

Indicative content guidance Answers must be credited by using the level descriptors (below) in line with the general marking guidance. The indicative content below exemplifies some of the points that candidates may make but this does not imply that any of these must be included. Other relevant points must also be credited. Knowledge, Application, Analysis, Evaluation – indicative content • Small businesses may need to find ways in which to compete, other than on price • This is due to the ability of larger firms to receive more discounts on costs and thus to charge lower prices • Ways in which small businesses may compete include developing a market niche, being flexible, offering a personal service, providing better communication and through innovation • Golcar Fisheries sells quality food which has led to a good reputation locally, therefore it is likely to be able to retain its customers and attract new ones • The business has a reputation for helping the local community, for example by offering free meals for school children during school holidays • This may mean Golcar Fisheries attracts more customers at these times because parents may accompany the children and buy meals too • As a result, revenue may increase and even lead to a higher profit, if the additional revenue made outweighs the cost of the food given away • However, as costs of food and fuel are increasing, customers may not be able to afford to buy food from takeaways • This may lead to fewer customers for Golcar Fisheries, meaning lower revenue and less profit • As a result, the business may not be able to compete with other shops that sell hot food in the area, especially it the competitors have more financial backing • Nonetheless, the higher food and fuel prices will affect other businesses too, meaning competitors may struggle to remain competitive if they focus on financial methods of doing so • This may mean that using ways to compete such as offering a personal service and helping the local
